coredata.dc:title: RETRACTED: Carbon dot/TAT peptide co-conjugated bubble nanoliposome for multicolor cell imaging, nuclear-targeted delivery, and chemo/photothermal synergistic therapy

coredata.dc:description: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal ( https://www.elsevier.com/about/our-business/policies/article-withdrawal ). This article has been retracted at the request of the Editor following concerns raised by a reader. Figure 1F is composed of copies of the same set of nine particles of various orientations. Also the article uses an electron micrograph in Figure 2A which represents a different part of the same image shown in Figure 3B from the paper published by the authors in вҖңBiosensors and BioelectronicsвҖқ 86 (2016) 818вҖ“826 http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2016.07.084 while claiming to represent an entirely different type of nanomaterial. The bottom of Figure 2A corresponds to the top of Figure 3B. The explanation provided by the corresponding author for this similarity was considered not satisfactory by the Editor. These problems with the data cast doubt on all the data, and accordingly also the conclusions based on that data, in this publication. As such this article represents a severe abuse of the scientific publishing system. The scientific community takes a very strong view on this matter and apologies are offered to readers of the journal that this was not detected during the submission process.

The ntial inArticle history: Received 26 August 2016 Received in revised form 18 November 2016 Accepted 19 November 2016 Available online 24 November 2016 Keywords: A pioneering nucleus-targeting dual-functional thermosensitive bubble-generating liposome as drug car- rier has been developed with conjugated cell penetrating peptide and photothermal agent (CDs) for multi-color cell imaging and combination (chemo- and photothermal) therapy of cancer, using doxoru- bicin as drug molecule. The liposome was prepared by ГўвӮ¬Лңin-houseГўвӮ¬в„ў synthesized precursors molecules, to reduce the cost of production in comparison to the earlier reported liposomes. This drug carrier was pro- posed to target nucleus of the cancer cells, owing to specific and selective cell-penetrating property of TAT, followed by burst but stable drug release due to decomposition of bubble forming agent present Tbeen reported. CDs have been incorporated for optical monitoring as well as NIR absorbing agent. CDs/TAT@NBLs enabled the optical monitoring of the cells multicolor fluorescence. Biocompatible, economic & stable liposome shows improved therapeutic efficacy. It is a multifunctional platform with low cytotoxicity and efficient cancer cell-killing. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c tR A C Designing of CDs & TAT conjugated multifunctional bubble liposome hasCarbon dot/TAT peptid multicolor cell imagin photothermal synergis Ekta Roy a, Santanu Patra a, R aDepartment of Applied Chemistry, Indian Insti b Functional Nanomaterials Research Laboratory h i g h l i g h t s-conjugated bubble nanoliposome for uclear-targeted delivery, and chemo/ therapy i Madhuri a,ГўвҖЎвҖҳ, Prashant K. Sharma b echnology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826 004, India ment of Applied Physics, Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826 004, India g r a p h i c a l a b s t r a c t rnal homepage: www.elsevier .com/locate /ce j E D have reported the liposomes bearing a photolabile-caged peptide for targeted delivery into cancer cells [10]. In our previous work, ring A1. Introduction The first and foremost requirement to treat incurable diseases is targeted delivery of appropriate drugs using an efficient drug car- rier. Various nanoforms have already been used as drug delivery system, starting from albumin, gelatin, metals, oxides, semicon- ductors, polymers, liposomes to nanomagnets [1ГўвӮ¬вҖң3]. However, most of them start their journey from blood vessels and end them in the close vicinity of a tumor cell or at the cell membrane, extreme to the cytoplasm and very rarely reached to the nucleus. However, the main target of these carriers was tumor cell nucleus, which should be destroyed to actually kill the disease. For this, the anti-cancer drugs are required to enter the cell nucleus, but in a real situation, it is very difficult for drugs to remain in their active form after arriving at the nucleus [4,5]. Therefore, we required a nucleus targeted drug delivery system (NTDDs) to enhance the effi- cacy of currently available drugs. Temperature-sensitive liposomes (TSLs) and/or polymer- modified thermosensitive liposomes (PTSLs) are the most com- monly employed nanosystem for drug delivery owing to their excellent pharmacokinetic profiles, low toxicity, membrane com- patibility and hyperthermia-triggered site-specific drug release mechanism [6]. Some of them have already entered into the mar- ket, for example, Doxil , Daunoxome for doxorubicin delivery. Drug release from TSLs/PTSLs can be induced by a very mild increase in the temperature of the specific site i.e. 39ГўвӮ¬вҖң40 C (few degrees above physiological temperature) [7,8]. The anciently used sources of hyperthermia to trigger drug release from TSLs are microwave, IR laser, or radiofrequency heating, which require the specific handling and painful for the patients too [9ГўвӮ¬вҖң11]. In addi- tion, the TSLs are also subject to some limitations including fast burst release of drugs, low encapsulation efficiency, poor storage stability and lack of tunable triggers for drug release [12]. But the main disadvantage lies with the liposomes is their low perme- ation through tumor cells resulting in drug release in the extracel- lular fluid. To make the liposomes specifically targeted to the nucleus, they must be functionalized with the proper targeting ligand like nuclear localization signaling (NLS) peptides. In spite of this, the liposome carriers are unable to a show the fluorescence property; therefore, they should also be attached with some fluo- rescent dye for tracking up their drug delivery path and process. In the recent years, liposomes were well modified with various tar- geting as well as fluorescence probes, to make them multi- functional [13]. For example, recently, Yu et al. have designed a tumor targeting and locally triggered-release nanocarrier system based on gold nanorods (GNRs) and thermosensitive bubble lipo- somes (TSBLs) [14]. Herein, GNRs were used to absorb near infra- red (NIR) radiation and polyethylene glycol (PEG) for targeting and better stability in the blood [14]. The GNRs can able to absorb NIR light and convert it into heat, resulting in an elevated temper- ature of nanocarrier and drug release. The elevated temperature and drug release synergistically kill the tumor cells and lower down the anti-cancer drug dose, which is well known for their side effects. Similarly, Qin et al. have also reported an NIR-triggered drug delivery system based on the amphiphilic chitosan derivative-coated single-wall carbon nanotubes (CNT) encapsu- lated in the poly(N-isopropyl acrylamide). After that PEG diacrylate was applied in the present work to tune the nanoparticles with the phase transition temperature [15]. Another work is reported which is based on the dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC) liposome, with gold nanoparticle and poly(N-isopropyl acrylamide-co-butyl methacrylate). This modified liposome was used to study the release profile of calcein [16]. E. Roy et al. / Chemical Enginee R E T RTo increase the target specificity and improved cell permeation, some of the researchers have also employed the cell-penetratingwe have also tried to use R9 and carbon dots modified liposome for transdermal delivery of curcumin. The result shows that the R9 modified nanosized liposome has greater stability, long circula- tion time with higher cytocompatibility than conventional liposomes. Herein, for the first time, we have developed a carbon dot (CDs) and TAT-conjugated bubble liposome (CDs/TAT@NBLs) for multiple applications i.e. multi-color cell imaging, nucleus-targeting, NIR- triggered drug delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) and NIR photother- mal therapy, simultaneously. For nucleus targeting, we have used the most popular cell-penetrating peptides i.e. TAT and CDs were used as NIR absorbing nanomaterial to trigger the drug release and also involve in the photothermal killing of tumor cells. In addi- tion to these, to reduce the overall cost of liposome synthesis, we have used some laboratory prepared precursors, derived from very cost-effective chemicals/reagents, namely, (1) (1,3- distearamidopropan-2-yl) ethyl 2-((2- aminoethyl) dimethylam- monio) phosphate (DSAEP), (2) (1,3-dipalmitamidopropan-2-yl) ethyl 2-((4-aminophenyl)dimethyl ammonio) phosphate (DPAEP), and (3) 2,2-dimethyl-3-tetradecanamidopropyl (2-((tris(2- hydroxypropyl) ammonio) oxy) ethyl) phosphate (EAHEP). The synthesis process of all the precursors was shown in various Schemes (Schemes 1 and 2). The CDs/TAT@NBLs DDS constructed by the non-covalent grafting of anticancer drug (DOX), not only efficiently accelerated nuclear and tumor accumulation of DOX, but also markedly enhanced the cytotoxicity for cancer cells in both conditions i.e. in vitro and in vivo, which is found superior to many other nanoparticle/liposome-based DOX delivery systems. The proposed liposome shows several advantages over the com- monly reported drug carriers like: (1) The CDs/TAT@NBLs was stable enough for circulation in the blood without any leakage and targeted to reach the cell nuclei by the help of TAT peptide; (2) NIR-mediated heat stimulus generated via CDs shows dual per- formance i.e. increases the tumor cell temperature and triggered the drug release via bursting of bubble present inside the lipo- somes; (3) CDs modification enables the liposomes for proper and multi-colored cell imaging. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials and instrumentation 2-Chloro-1,3,2-dioxaphospholane-2-oxide (COP), tri-isopropanol amine, N,NГўвӮ¬в„ў-dimethyl paraphenylenediamine, TAT, N,N0-Dicyclo hexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and DOX were purchased from Sigma- Aldrich (India) and TCI chemicals (India). Carboxymethylcellulose, myristic acid, thionyl chloride, stearic acid, cholesterol, palmitic acid, triethylamine (TEA), glacial acetic acid and the solvents like methanol, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethyl sulphoxide C T Epeptides (CPPs) modified liposomes. The CPPs (example R9, TAT etc.) are derived from proteins and able to cross biological mem- branes very efficiently. Among the various types of CPPs, the TAT peptides are one of the most promising and most studied CPPs. According to Pan et al. TAT peptide has been shown to be an effi- cient molecule for translocating nanoparticles into cell nuclei via the binding import receptors importin a and b (karyopherin) and subsequently targeting the nuclear pore complexes of cancer cells and entering their nuclei [17]. Recently, Yang et al. have reported NIR-responsive liposome which is modified with asparagine- glycine-arginine peptide for targeted drug delivery [9]. Xie et al. Roy et al. / Chemical EngineeThe different conjugates of liposome were characterized by FT- IR spectra by using a Varian Fourier Transform Infrared [FT-IR (USA)] spectrometer recorded in the range of 400ГўвӮ¬вҖң4000 cm 1. The particle sizes of the liposome at different temperature were measured using a dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Zeta sizer nano-S90, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) with a HeГўвӮ¬вҖңNe laser beam at a wavelength of 633.8 nm. Measurements were made at 5 C intervals using a temperature range of 25ГўвӮ¬вҖң50 C. A delay time of 5 min was used at each temperature to ensure that the sample viscosity was equilibrated before the measurements were taken. The temperature-sensitive behavior, melting point temperature measurement, and optical density (O.D.) of the liposomes were evaluated with a UV Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Lambda 35, Singapore). The O.D. was measured over a temperature range of 25ГўвӮ¬вҖң50 C. For the UVГўвӮ¬вҖңVis and DLS study, 1.0 mL of lipo- some suspension was taken in another 2.5 mL of PBS/ABC and the measurements were taken. Confocal images were acquired using a Zeiss confocal laser scanning unit mounted on an LSM 710 fixed-stage upright microscope. Morphological images of the liposome were recorded using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), Zeiss, model Supra 55 and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analyses were per- formed on Bruker D8 Focus X-ray diffractometer, and Tecnai T-30 (300 kV FEG TEM). The apparent critical micelle concentration (CMC) was deter- mined using pyrene as a fluorescent probe. For this, 1.0 lM con- centration of pyrene was (dissolved in acetone) added in different empty glass vials and left for the evaporation of acetone. In the vial, various concentrations of liposome solutions were added and entrapment of pyrene in the liposome was determined by steady-state fluorescence spectra recorded by excitation at Scheme 1. Schematic illustration showing the synthesis R E T R AJournal 312 (2017) 144ГўвӮ¬вҖң157 T E D390 nm. The characteristics emission ratio of pyrene i.e. ratio of intensities of the first and third vibronic peaks (I1/I3) are very con- sidered to estimate the polarity level of their environment [18]. Hereby, we declare that all the animal experiments were per- formed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines and the Institute of Medical Sciences, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi has approved the experiments. 2.2. Preparation of liposome precursors In this work, we have synthesized three different lipid mole- cules conjugated with CDs and TAT using the previously reported procedure from our laboratory with slight modification [6]. The chemical structure of three synthesized lipids i.e. (1) (1,3- distearamidopropan-2-yl) ethyl 2-((2- aminoethyl) dimethylam- monio) phosphate (DSAEP) (2) (1,3-dipalmitamidopropan-2-yl) ethyl 2-((4-aminophenyl)dimethylammonio) phosphate (DPAEP), and (3) 2,2-dimethyl-3-tetradecanamidopropyl (2-((tris(2- hydroxypropyl) ammonio) oxy) ethyl) phosphate (EAHEP) were given in Schemes 1 and 2. In addition, the bioconjugate of choles- terol and CDs (chol@CDs) was also prepared as a labeling agent. The preparation process was given below in a step-wise manner: 2.2.1. Synthesis of DSAEP and TAT-DSAEP bioconjugate For the synthesis of DSAEP, firstly, 1,3-diamino-2-propanol (3.92 mmol, 0.35 g) and 0.3 mL TEA were dissolved in 6.0 mL of THF, taken in 100.0 mL round bottom flask and placed in ice- bath. To the mixture, COP (3.92 mmol in 6.0 mL THF) was added in a drop-wise manner with continuous stirring, resulting in a white precipitate of 2-((1,3-diaminopropan-2-yl)oxy)-1,3,2-dioxa phospholane 2-oxide (APDPO), which is recrystallized with of liposome precursors: (A) DPAEP and (B) EAHEP. C ringE. Roy et al. / Chemical Engineemethanol for further use. After that, 0.076 g (0.04 mmol) of recrys- tallized APDPO was taken in 10.0 mL methanol and mixed and refluxed with stearoyl chloride (0.012 g, 0.04 mmol in 5.0 mL methanol) for 12 h at 50 C. The resulting product N,N0-(2-((2-oxi Scheme 2. (A) Chemical structure of all the constituents used in the preparation of C liposome precursor i.e. TAT-DSAEP. For next step, 0.05 g (0.06 mmol) of DPDA was mixed with N,N0 dimethylene ethylene diamine (0.06 mmol, 0.005 g). The mixture was refluxed for 12 h Ds/TAT@NBLs; (B) Schematic illustration showing the synthesis of TAT-conjugate C T E D tion used for the preparation of different liposomes was summa- lyzing against 20 wt% sucrose solution with 5.0 mM NaCl, free PBS ature. Each condition was tested in triplicate. After this, the ring Aat 50 C and the resulting lipid (1,3-distearamidopropan-2-yl) ethyl 2-((2- aminoethyl) dimethylammonio) phosphate (DSAEP) was conjugated with TAT peptide. TAT peptide was covalently conjugated with the ГўвӮ¬вҖңNH2 function- alized lipid molecule through ГўвӮ¬вҖңCOOH group by the help of cross-linking reagents EDC and NHS. For this, 0.1 mmol TAT was dissolved in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and mixed with 0.1 mmol EDC and 0.25 mmol NHS followed by stirring for 15ГўвӮ¬вҖң20 min to activate the ГўвӮ¬вҖңCOOH group present in TAT peptide. To this solution, DSAEP lipid (0.20 g, 0.25 mmol) was added and the mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. After this, the TAT-modified DSAEP was centrifuged, collected and kept in a desiccator. 2.2.2. Synthesis of (1,3-dipalmitamidopropan-2-yl) ethyl 2-((4- aminophenyl)dimethylammonio) phosphate (DPAEP) For the synthesis of the second peptide (DPAEP), 0.077 g (0.3 mmol) of the above-synthesized APDPO was taken, dissolved in 2.0 mL methanol and mixed with N,N0-dimethyl para- phenylenediamine (0.04 g, 0.3 mmol dissolved in 2.0 mL metha- nol) [6]. The mixture was refluxed at 50 C for 12 h resulting in a black liquid intermediate product i.e. 1,3-diaminopropan-2-yl (2- ((4-aminophenyl)dimethylammonio) ethyl) phosphonate, (APDEP). Furthermore, 0.09 g (0.2 mmol) of APDEP was refluxed with 0.2 mmol of palmitoyl chloride (0.05 g dissolved in 5.0 mL methanol) for 24 h, results in the formation of DPAEP. The synthe- sized lipid was washed with charcoal and recrystallized with methanol. 2.2.3. Synthesis of 2,2-dimethyl-3-tetradecanamidopropyl (2-((tris(2- hydroxypropyl) ammonio) oxy) ethyl) phosphate (EAHEP) For the synthesis of EAHEP, the similar procedure as mentioned above was adopted with different reagent and chemicals. Firstly, 3.92 mmol of 3-amino-2,2-dimethyl-1propanol and 0.54 mL (3.92 mmol) TEA were taken in 100.0 mL round bottom flask con- taining 6.0 mL of THF and placed in an ice-bath. After that, 3.92 mmol of COP was dropwise added to the mixture with contin- uous stirring. A white precipitate of 2-(3-amino-2,2-dimethylpro poxy)-1,3,2-dioxaphospholane 2-oxide (ADDPO) was appeared, which is centrifuged and purified for next step. After that, 0.137 g (0.6 mmol) of ADDPO was dissolved in 2.0 mL methanol and mixed with 1,10,100-nitrilotris(propan-2-ol) (0.114g, 0.6 mmol dissolved in 2.0 mL methanol) and the mixture was refluxed at 50 C for 12 h, which gives a colorless liquid [3-amino-2,2-dime thylpropyl(2-((tris(2-hydroxypropyl)ammonio)oxy)ethyl)phospho nate,APHEP], after completion of reaction. To the liquid, 2.0 mmol of tetradecanoyl chloride (dissolved in 5.0 mL methanol) was added and further refluxed for 24 h, resulting in third lipid i.e. EAHEP. The compound was recrystallized with methanol and stored in a vacuum desiccator for further use. 2.2.4. Synthesis of CDs and cholesterol conjugate CDs (Chol@CDs) In a typical synthesis of CDs, 0.5 g of carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) was dissolved in 25.0 mL of water and mixed with 2.0 mL of 0.01 M H2SO4, under vigorous stirring. The mixture was then subjected to microwave irradiation for 30 min at 750 W to obtain a precipitate and a light-brown supernatant. The supernatant liq- uid and the precipitate were separated by centrifugation, and the carbon dots (CDs) were collected from the supernatant by dialysis for 24 h. The collected CDs were further concentrated by evapora- tion of the solvent to enhance their fluorescence property. For the bioconjugation of CDs and cholesterol, 15.0 mL of CDs 148 E. Roy et al. / Chemical Enginee R E T Rsolution was added in the 0.33 mmol methanol solution of choles- terol under nitrogen atmosphere and kept for stirring at 60 C for 7 h. After that, the solvent was evaporated by Rota-evaporatorsuspension was dialyzed against pure water to separate the DOX- loaded liposome from free DOX present in the solution. DOX con-or ABC was removed. The as prepared liposomes were then freeze- dried and stored at 4 C for further use. The liposome prepared by conventional precursors was termed as conventional liposomes, with and without bubbles i.e. CDs/TAT@CBLs and CDs/TAT@CLs. The liposomes prepared by new precursors were termed as CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@NLs (in the presence and absence of bubble). In order to explore the effect of each additional constituents (i.e. CDs and TAT), the new liposomes were also syn- thesized without using them i.e. CBLs, NBLs, CLs and NLs (without carbon dot and TAT peptide). Similarly, a liposome without TAT (CDs@NBLs) was also prepared to demonstrate the nuclear target- ing ability of liposomes. 2.4. Drug loading and in vitro drug release from the liposome For drug loading, DOX was mixed with the liposome suspension at a lipid/drug ratio of 0.05 (w/w) and sonicated at 37 C for 30 min and the free drug was removed from liposomes by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The amount of DOX-loaded into the lipo- somes was determined using UVГўвӮ¬вҖңVisible spectroscopy. The encap- sulation efficiency (% EE) and encapsulation content (% EC) of the liposomes was measured using the given equations: % EEГӮВј ГғВ°mass of DOX in liposome=mass of DOX in initial solutionГғЕҫ 100% ГғВ°1ГғЕҫ % EC ГӮВј ГғВ°mass of DOX in liposome=mass of lipidsГғЕҫ 100% ГғВ°2ГғЕҫ For release studies, doxorubicin-loaded liposomes were added in a glass cuvette, which optical density (O.D.) was measured at 500 nm. The cuvette was placed in three conditions: (1) 5 cm away from NIR laser source (810 nm, 2 W cm 2, Power Technology, AR, USA) for 10 min, (2) at 45 C temperature, and (3) at body temper- Crized in Table 1. All the liposomes were synthesized by the same process i.e. thin film hydration method [16,19], but different com- position and different solvent medium like for bubble liposome preparation ammonium bicarbonate (ABC) is used, however, the without liposome was prepared by phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The synthesis process of liposome with CDs and TAT peptide is as follows: 0.10 mmol of TAT-DSAEP, 0.25 mmol of DPAEP, 0.25 mmol of EAHEP, 0.10 mmol cholesterol and 0.10 mmol of Chol@CDs were dissolved in chloroform and solution was stirred for 15 min. After that, the solvent was evaporated by rotary evap- oration resulting in the formation of thin film of dry lipid in the flask. The lipid film was hydrated using PBS or ABC via sonication at room temperature for 30 min followed by sequential extrusion through 500 nm polycarbonate filters (six times). Through the dia- E Dand the resulting product (Chol@CDs) was recrystallized in metha- nol and stored in vacuum desiccators, before further use. 2.3. Liposome preparation In this study, the standard liposome was also prepared for con- trol study using the conventional lipid precursors i.e. dimyristoyl phosphatidic acid (DMPA), dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC), cholesterol taken as conventional liposomes. Roy et al. / Chemical Engineein a cycle of 3 min on, 4 min off, and 3 min on. At each break, 0.5 mL of solution was removed and their DOX content was analyzed. 2.5. In vitro stability study To investigate the stability of prepared liposome, Triton X-100 surfactant was used. The non-ionic surfactant like Triton X-100 is widely used as a good solubilization agent of the lipid membranes [20,21]. Herein, different concentrations of Triton X-100 were used to study their effect on the stability of prepared liposome. Firstly, in the 10.0 mL solutions of liposome different concentrations of Triton X-100 (0.1ГўвӮ¬вҖң25 mM) were added and incubated for 12 h, thereafter the corresponding O.D. of the solution was measured by UVГўвӮ¬вҖңVis spectrophotometry. The % loss in O.D. after the addition of Triton X-100 can give the % solubilization of lipid membrane. For the serum stability study, a DulbeccoГўвӮ¬в„ўs modified eagle media (DMEM) with a concentration of 50% (m/v) BSA was pre- pared and used as an in vitro serummodel to mimic the in vivo sta- tus. The as prepared liposomes were added to this serum model at 37 C for 72 h and DLS was used to measure the change in the size of liposomes after different time intervals (0ГўвӮ¬вҖң72 h). Similarly, the storage ability of liposomes was also determined by keeping the prepared liposome for the 6-month period of time in a 25.0 mL glass tube (amber-color) at room temperature. 2.6. Cell culture and in vitro cellular uptake The human breast cancer cell lines, MCF-7 were maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 100 lg mL 1 streptomycins. Cells were cultivated at 37 C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. After that, the imaging was performed in two ways: (1) the culture medium was replaced by fresh medium con- taining the PBS as control or the different DOX-free liposomes (with or without TAT-peptides) i.e. NBLs, CBLs, CDs/TAT@CBLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs. The liposomal formulations added medium was maintained at 37 C and the fluorescent images of cells were ana- lyzed using a laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus), to visualize the cellular intake of prepare liposomes. The multi-color cell imaging was performed using diode lasers with a wavelength of 400 nm, 480 nm and 560 nm for the excitation. In the second mode of imaging, DOX-loaded liposomes (DOX-NBLs, DOX-CBLs, DOX-CDs/TAT@CBLs, DOX-CDs/TAT@NBLs) were incubated with the MCF-7 cells and their imaging were performed to see the drug release profiles of different liposomes at 42 and 37 C. For a tem- perature increase the NIR laser source was used (Scheme 3). 2.7. In vitro cytotoxicity Cytotoxicity of each formulation was evaluated by MTT assay with MCF-7 cells. The cells were seeded into a 96-well plate for 24 h (1 103 cells/well) and incubated at 37 C with 5% CO2. The supernatants were discarded, and the cells were washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4). To the culture medium, Free DOX, a DOX- loaded liposome (NBLs, CBLs, CDs/TAT@CBLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs) having DOX concentration of 10, 25, 50, 75, or 100 lg mL 1, respectively and blank liposomes (without DOX) formulations were added and incubated for 2 h. The PBS solution was also added to the separate well for control analysis. After this, 15.0 lL of MTT solution (5.0 mg mL 1) was added to each well, and the cells were further incubated for 2 h at 37 C. Then, the medium was removed and 100.0 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to dissolve C ring Athe formazan crystals formed by the living cells, followed by mea- surement of O.D. value by UVГўвӮ¬вҖңvisible spectrophotometer. Roy et al. / Chemical Enginee Rtest (p-value 6 0.05). 3. Result and discussion 3.1. Characterization of carbon dots The synthesized CDs were well characterized by various tech- niques like PXRD, FT-IR, UV, TEM and PL and shown in Fig. 1. The PXRD pattern of CDs shows a single peak at 18.1 (d = 6.85 nm), which corresponds to the 002 plane of the graphite structure [22]. However, the broad peaks suggest the amorphous nature of the prepared CDs (Fig. 1-A) [22]. The UVГўвӮ¬вҖңVis absorption spectrum of CDs shows a sharp absorption at about 240 nm, which is ascribed to the p-pГўпҝҪвҖһ transition and attributed to the presence of AC@O functional groups at the surface of CDs (Fig. 1-B). In order to study the functional groups present at the surface of CDs, their FT-IR spectrum was recorded. The spectrum shows an absorption peak at 3423 cm 1 is attributed the presence of AOH group. How- ever, the peak at 2067 and 1633 cm 1 may be due to the ACH and AC@O stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 1-C). To explore the fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs, detailed PL study was carried out at different excitation wavelengths (kex). As shown in Fig. 1-D, when the kex increases from 240 nm to 460 nm, the emis- sion peak gradually increases and shifted towards longer wave- lengths (red shift). After 460 nm, the peak intensity starts decreasing but the red-shifting continued and at an excitation R E Twavelength of 530 nm the intensity becomes very low, which indi- cate a distribution of the different surface energy traps of the CDs [23]. The maximum PL emission in this range was found with exci- tation of 460 nm (kex). Furthermore, when the CDs were excited with low-energy radiation (NIR light) in the range of 980 to 660 nm, the emission was obtained in the range of 440ГўвӮ¬вҖң450 nm, with good PL intensity, which clearly supports the upconversion PL properties of prepared CDs (Fig. 1-E). The results indicate that prepared CDs shows a kex tunable emission with excellent upcon- verted PL properties as well as strong luminescence in visible-to- NIR range. The PL quantum yield of the CDs was determined to be 18.8% by using rhodamine B as a standard. The morphological characterization of CDs was carried out by HR-TEM images (Fig. 1-F) and it was found that the prepared CDs are spherical in shape with a diameter in the range of 5ГўвӮ¬вҖң10 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CDs shows broadly diffused rings with only one or two bright spots, which supports the XRD data and amorphous nature of CDs. The stability of prepared CDs was well studied in different con- ditions like a change in ionic strength of the aqueous medium, effect of UV-light illumination, pH of the solution (Fig. S1). In the study, it was found that no change was observed in the PL intensity of prepared CDs, even in the presence of high ionic strength med- ium i.e. 2.0 M NaCl, which supports their high stability. Similarly, with a change in pH, either in the acidic or alkaline side, negligible change in PL intensity was observed. In addition, the CDs solution was irradiated with UV light for 60 min, and approximately 1% change in PL intensity was observed. While storing the CDs solu- tion at room temperature for 3 months, no obvious change in PL intensity was observed. The results are in good consequence with the high stability of the prepared CNDs. 3.2. Characterization of liposome The entire lipid precursors used in this work, have been prop- erly characterized by FT-IR and 1H NMR analysis and discussed in Supporting Information (Figs. S2ГўвӮ¬вҖңS9). The schematic representa- tion showing the synthesis of used precursors was given in the Schemes 1 and 2. Including conventional and new liposomes, in this work; we have fabricated eight (08) different types of lipo- somes using different components and solvent medium. Table 1 has listed the composition of each liposomal formulation and important characteristics of the liposomes, including mean diame- ter, EE, EC, lower critical solution temperature (LCST) and polydis- persity index (PDI) value. All of the prepared liposomes showed good homogeneity, with PDIs below 0.20 and average particle sizes between 100ГўвӮ¬вҖң165 nm. The fabricated liposomes were of appropri- ate size for their transportation in the vasculature of the tumor tis- sue and could easily accumulate at the tumor site by the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. As portrayed in the Table, the new liposomes possess the comparatively low size and high encapsulation efficiency in comparison to the conventional lipo- somes, which supports their future application (in vivo) in very cost-effective manner. In addition to these, as shown in Table 1, all the liposomes show positive zeta potential, owing to the pres- ence of positively charged amino groups in the lipid molecule. However, the liposomes having TAT-conjugated lipid shows more positive zeta potential value than that of without TAT-conjugated liposome, owing to the presence of cationic charges of the TAT- peptide. The morphology of prepared liposome (CDs/TAT@NBLs) was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and field emis- sion scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) techniques (Fig. 2-A Journal 312 (2017) 144ГўвӮ¬вҖң157 C T E Dand B). The regular, homogeneously distributed spherical shape of the liposomes is visible in both the images having a size in the range of 80ГўвӮ¬вҖң90 nm, which is slightly larger than the hydrodynamic . (D) ED E. Roy et al. / Chemical Engineering Journal 312 (2017) 144ГўвӮ¬вҖң157 151diameter of the liposome reported via DLS study. The SAED pattern of liposome shows their amorphous nature in Fig. 2-C. Furthermore, to explore the photothermal effect of all prepared liposomes (CLs, CBLs, NLs, NBLs, CDs/TAT@CLs, CDs/TAT@CBLs, CDs/TAT@NLs and CDs/TAT@NBLs) their temperature change was measured under laser irradiation. For such purpose, water was used as a control. When all the liposomes were placed 5 cm near Fig. 1. (A) XRD pattern, (B) UVГўвӮ¬вҖңvisible, and (C) FT-IR spectrum of synthesized CDs different excitation wavelengths. (F) TEM image of CDs with their magnified and SAto the laser source (NIR), the maximum temperature raised to 65ГўвӮ¬вҖң68 C in all CDs containing liposomes. While the liposome without CDs did not shows any significant rise in the temperature (Fig. 2-D). The study demonstrates that the CDs conjugated lipo- somes could able to lead the irreversible cell damage (hyperther- mia) in the presence of NIR radiation [24]. 3.3. The liposome stability In the field of drug delivery, the long-term stability of drug encapsulated liposome is a bigger task and major challenge for the researchers. Firstly, the stability of prepared liposomes was investigated during their storage at room temperature. During the storage, no obvious change in their physical appearance and diameter occurs after 3 months, only some aggregation of particles was appeared (Table 2). During the storage period, the encapsula- tion efficiency (%) was also evaluated and found that %EE of CDs/TAT@NBLs remains somewhat same ( 98%) after three months interval (Table 2), while the encapsulation efficiencies of NBLs, decreases to 89ГўвӮ¬вҖң86%. The conventional liposome also shows a similar trend, where the CBLs show 85ГўвӮ¬вҖң88% EE, the CLs decreases to 70ГўвӮ¬вҖң65%. The results of the stability experiments show that TAT modification makes the liposome more stable than the liposomes without TAT and the newly synthesized precursors lead to more stable liposome than the conventional one. In a different study, the stability of liposome in the presence of Triton X-100 and serum medium was also investigated. The multi- layer stability study was done against Triton X-100, a nonionic sur- R E T R A factant that has a hydrophilic polyethylene oxide chain and an aromatic hydrocarbon hydrophobic group. It is reported that when Triton X-100 interacts with liposomes, the vesicle-micelle transi- tion involved and induces the solubilization of lipid vesicles caus- ing deformation of liposomal membrane structure. DLS study was used to measure the change in diameter of liposomes in the pres- ence of different concentrations of Triton X-100. As shown in Photoluminescence (PL) and (E) Upconverted PL spectra of the CDs obtained with images in the inset. C T E DFig. 2-E, the average diameter of the liposomes gradually decreased by Triton X-100, indicating deformation of the membrane. How- ever, the CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs show a minimum change in their diameter, despite the addition of Triton X-100. This may be due to the formation of bilayer in both the liposomes, which increases the physical stability. The lowest diameter change in CDs/TAT@NBLs is may be due to the incorporation of CPP in a lipid membrane, which further enhances the membrane stability. To further analyze the stability of as prepared liposomes, their serum stability was also studied and shown in the Fig. 2-F. For this, the prepared liposomes were incubated in the DMEM and their hydrodynamic diameters were measured by DLS. As shown in the image, slight change in the diameter of CDs/TAT@CBLs was observed with change in the time. However, almost negligible change in the diameter was obtained for CDs/TAT@NBLs. The sta- bility study successfully supports the higher stability of newly syn- thesized liposomes in comparison to the conventional liposome. Recently, to verify the stability of prepared liposome solution, their critical aggregation concentration (CAC) was calculated. CAC is the concentration above which the intermolecular hydrogen bonding, micelles, or other aggregates start forming i.e. lower the CAC the stronger the micelles upon dilution. For such purpose, pyr- ene was used as a probe molecule. This point could be calculated by finding the point of intersection of the tangents of fluorescence curve shown in the Fig. S10 and the CAC value of CDs/TAT@NBLs was determined to be about 53.70 mg L 1. The value is comparable enough to the standard liposomes reported in the literature, which supports their stability in the aqueous medium also [6]. Fig. 2. Roy et al. / Chemical Engineering Journal 312 (2017) 144ГўвӮ¬вҖң157 R E T R A C T E D ring A3.4. Temperature sensitive and NIR-responsive behavior of liposomes The temperature-sensitive behavior of liposomes (CDs/ TAT@NLs, CDs/TAT@CLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs) was studied by DLS method. When the temperature was increased up to 45 C (the phase transition temperature of liposomes) the aver- age diameter of the liposomes decreases significantly (shown in Table 2). As expected, the diameter of bubble liposomes shows more decrease in comparison to the without bubble liposomes. Because in bubble liposomes (TAT@NBLs and TAT@CBLs), there is additional triggering mechanism is present i.e. bubble formation. According to the literature, at the temperature of or above 40 C, ABC starts decomposes into water, ammonia and CO2 bubbles and the decomposition mechanism of aqueous ABC was second order, an irreversible reaction which leads to the faster breaking of liposome bilayer, results in an enormous decrease in diameter of bubble liposome. To explore the effect of NIR radiation on liposomes, the DLS study was again performed in the presence of laser source. For this, the liposome solutions were exposed to the NIR-laser source for 5 min and the change in diameter were observed. The correspond- ing results were portrayed in Table 2. Here, similar behavior of liposomes was found as they have depicted with a change in the temperature. This supports that carbon dots attached with lipo- some are sufficient enough to increase the inside temperature of liposome and also confirms that synthesized liposomes were NIR sensitive. 3.5. Drug loading capacity The drug loading capacity in liposomes was studied by thin film/sonication method. In Table 1, the % encapsulation efficiency (EE) and % encapsulation content (EC) of all prepared liposome (CLs, CBLs, NLs, NBLs, CDs/TAT@NLs, CDs/TAT@CLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs) were showed. The %EE and %EC show similar profile for all the liposomes and summarized below: CLs < NLs < CDs=TAT@CLs < CDs=TAT@NLs < CBLs < NBLs < CDs=TAT@CBLs < CDs=TAT@NBLs From the Table 1 and above shown trend, it can be easily con- cluded that bubble liposomes have higher %EE and %EC values as compared to the without-bubble liposomes. It was also found that the newly synthesized liposomes show high encapsulation effi- ciency (%) and encapsulation content (%) than the conventional liposomes. The highest value was observed for liposome having all the additional keys i.e. cells penetrating peptide, ABS as a sol- vent and newly synthesized precursors. The result could be explained by poor solubility of DOX molecules, which is highly lipophilic in nature [25]. In the conventional liposome, the drug remains loaded in the bilayer and did not enter the aqueous core, which may result in lower %EC and %EE values. However, in bubble liposome the drug may lead towards the core of the liposome resulting in high %EC and %EE values. 3.6. Drug release study 3.6.1. Temperature responsive behavior of prepared liposomes The release behavior of DOX from the CLs, CBLs, NLs, NBLs, CDs/TAT@NLs, CDs/TAT@CLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs were investigated using a dialysis membrane in the PBS medium at two different temperatures i.e. 37 and 45 C (Fig. 3). From the figure, it is clearly visualized that conventional liposomes (CLs E. Roy et al. / Chemical Enginee R E T Rand CDs/TAT@CLs) show lowest drug release efficiency at both the temperature (Fig. 3-A). At 37 C, they show 30% drug release, whereas, at 45 C, the value gets slightly increased and reached to50ГўвӮ¬вҖң60%, owing to the thermo-responsive behavior of liposomes [26]. However, the newly synthesized liposomes show very low drug release at 37 C (<8%) and 65ГўвӮ¬вҖң68% at 45 C. This supports the high stability and free from drug leakage property of newly synthesized liposomes. However, when the TAT-peptide was attached with new liposomes, their stability gets enhanced, and, therefore, the drug release at 37 C becomes almost negligible (less than 2%) (Fig. 3-B). In case of bubble containing liposomes (CBLs, NBLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs), when the temperature rose to 45 C, a burst release of drug was observed within 5ГўвӮ¬вҖң 6 min time span, owing to the simultaneous working of two trig- gering mechanism (i.e. temperature-sensitive behavior of liposome and bubble formation inside the core due to incorporated ABC, which gets burst and results in fast drug release) (Fig. 3-C). Among all of the synthesized liposomes, the CDs/TAT@CBLs shows maxi- mum drug release (95%) at 45 C in very small time span (6 min) and no-drug leakage at body temperature (37 C). This suggests the perfect drug carrier behavior of liposomes i.e. high stability, maximum drug loading capacity, and fast but stable drug release. 3.6.2. NIR-responsive behavior of prepared liposomes Fig. 3-D and E shows the effect of NIR radiation on the drug release profile of CLs, CBLs, NLs, NBLs, CDs/TAT@NLs, CDs/TAT@CLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs. For this, the drug release kinet- ics of prepared liposomes was compared in PBS medium of pH 7.4 (25 C), in the absence and presence of 5 min NIR light (power den- sity = 2.0 W cm 2) irradiation at the certain releasing time points of 0ГўвӮ¬вҖң5 s. In the absence of NIR radiation, the release of DOX from the conventional liposomes is very slow (10% after 5 min). How- ever, the new liposome doesnГўвӮ¬в„ўt show any drug release, in the absence of NIR radiation. After the 5 min irradiation of NIR light, a sharp increase in drug release was observed in the case of lipo- somes having carbon dots i.e. CDs/TAT@NLs, CDs/TAT@CLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs and approximately 85ГўвӮ¬вҖң98% of loaded DOX released within 5 min. However, almost negligible DOX release was observed with the liposomes without carbon dots conjugation. This result indicates that the CDs containing lipo- somes only possess the photothermal conversion ability in response to an NIR light irradiation. Due to NIR light irradiation, the fluorescent CDs embedded in the liposome produce local heat by the efficient photothermal conversion; which weaken the inter- actions between drug carrier-DOX and increase the mobility of DOX at elevated temperatures. But when the NIR irradiation was turned off, the CDs in the liposome could not produce local heat, thus, the drug release was not observed in the new liposomes, however, some slow release obtained in conventional liposomes may be contributed to their poor stability and drug leakage prob- lem. Therefore, from the above result, it can be easily concluded that CDs are essential and a key component to absorb the NIR radi- ation and convert it in the form of heat energy. 3.7. Cytotoxicity and in vitro photothermal therapy Cytotoxicity of the liposomes is an important consideration for materials intended for in vivo application. To investigate this, the cell viability of MCF-7 cancer cells was examined. After incubation, all the prepared liposomes (CDs/TAT@NLs, CDs/TAT@CLs, CDs/TAT@NBLs and CDs/TAT@CBLs) showed a minimal decrease in cell viability (<2%), when the cells were incubated with 0, 50 and 100.0 mg mL 1 of liposomes for 24 h (Fig. 4-A). Therefore, it can be concluded that the synthesized new liposomes are also hav- ing similar or better cytocompatibility with MCF-7 cells than con- ventional liposomes. This sup- ports the successful implementation of combination effect i.e. without CDs were also prepared and their confocal images were also recorded (Fig. S11). As shown in the images, no color was of DOX in the cells and the effect of the combination of chemo- and photothermal cancer therapy, in the presence and absence of NIR ring A chemo- and photothermal therapy for treatment of cancer cells. 3.8. In vivo photothermal effect Now a day, the photothermal therapy or use of infrared (IR) radiation have become very popular in the field of cancer therapy. It is convenient to use and heat the specific part of our body. IR radiation (comprised between 0.78 lm and 1 mm wavelength) is more useful than the visible radiation for heating our body, because we absorb most of it. When the skin was exposed to the radiation and internal heat of around 42ГўвӮ¬вҖң45 C temperature will be generated depending on the NIR agents, which is sufficient enough to kill the cancer cell and not harmful to the healthy cells. Lammertyn et al. have demonstrated that the depth of penetration of NIR radiation varied from 2ГўвӮ¬вҖң3 mm at 900ГўвӮ¬вҖң1900 nm to 4 mm at 700ГўвӮ¬вҖң900 nm [30]. In another study, the depth of penetration in a neonatal head ranged from 6.3ГўвӮ¬вҖң8.5 mm in the 700ГўвӮ¬вҖң900 nm spec- tral range [31]. For this reason, the infrared lamps used for thera- peutic purposes produce mainly 700ГўвӮ¬вҖң900 nm radiation, which can able to penetrate the tumor at their maximum extent. To assess the in vivo therapeutic potential of prepared liposome (CDs/TAT@NBLs), nude mice bearing the HeLa tumor model were injected intratumorally with 50.0 lL of aqueous dispersion of lipo- some (0.5 mg mL 1) or blank physiological saline, and the tumors were then exposed to 810 nm laser at a power density of 2 W cm 2 for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 3-F, the temperature of the tumor regions with liposome increased rapidly from 25 C to 65 C in 5 min only. In contrast, tumors injected with blank and laser only showed a temperature change of 3 C after the same laser irradi- ation treatment. The study clearly demonstrates that proposed liposome have the potential to increase the temperature inside R E T Rincubated with all the four liposomes at different concentrations (0ГўвӮ¬вҖң100.0 mg mL 1) for 24 h and then exposed to 810 nm NIR laser at a power density of 2 W cm 2 for 5 min. When the NIR radiation was used without CDs, the cell viability was observed ( 99%), which indicate that there was no effect of NIR radiation in the can- cer cell. However, when the same experiment was performed with CDs containing liposomes, even with 1000 times lower concentra- tion (i.e. 0ГўвӮ¬вҖң100.0 lg mL 1), higher inhibition efficiency on MCF-7 cells was observed (40.1 ГӮВұ 1.5%), which suggest the potential of prepared liposome for photothermal cancer therapy (Fig. 4-C). Presently, the combination therapy is very efficient in cancer therapy, for examples, photothermal-chemotherapy [27], photodynamic-chemotherapy [28], and combination therapy always play higher cancer treatment efficiency than any single treatment [29]. To explore the combination efficiency of prepared liposome for cancer treatment, the DOX-loaded liposomes were used (Fig. 4-D). For this, MCF-7 cells were incubated with free- DOX, DOX-loaded CBLs, NBLs, CDs/TAT@CBLs and CDs/TAT@NBLs for 24 h and then exposed to NIR at a power density of 2 W cm 2 for 5 min. There was no significant difference in cell viability was observed with free-DOX treated MCF-7 cells, due to the slow DOX release in the cancer cells. However, DOX-loaded CDs/TAT@CBLs and CDs/TAT@NBLs shows a good inhibition of can- cer cells growth. Among the four DOX-loaded liposomes, the CBLs and NBLs show the comparatively low killing ability of cancer cells, which could be explained by their inability to produce enough heat in the absence of CDs, which results in only chemotherapy. How- ever, with CDs/TAT@NBLs concentration of 100.0 lg mL 1, the inhibition rate significantly increased and cell viability reached to E. Roy et al. / Chemical Engineethe tumor cell of live mice and therefore possess real-time applica- tion in the field of photothermal therapy or treatment of cancer cells.radiation (Fig. 6). It has been observed that without NIR radiation, the DOX-loaded CDs/TAT@NBLs was easily penetrated through the cells but the cell killing rate is very slow and almost negligible cells were damaged. However, in the presence of NIR radiation, the kill- ing rate become very fast and approximately 90ГўвӮ¬вҖң95% cells were killed within a 5ГўвӮ¬вҖң10 min time span. The fast penetration and stable/rapid drug release are credited to the conjugation of TAT and CDs to the liposome surface. Therefore, we can conclude here, that synthesized liposome has great potential for multi-color cell- imaging, combination cancer therapy with effective cell penetrat- ing and rapid but stable drug release ability. 4. Conclusion In summary, we have reported the development of a multifunc- tional CDs/TAT@NBLs that possesses the multi-color cell-imaging, nucleus targeting, and in vivo therapeutic efficiency into a single carrier using newly synthesized, cost-effective precursors. The use of CDs is not limited to optical monitoring of the carrier but also used as an active component to absorb NIR radiation and facil- itate the drug delivery to the cell nucleus and tumors. The CDs modified liposomes have excitation-dependent tunable photolu- minescence, enabling optical monitoring of the cells in blue, green, and red fluorescence channels, which is not typically possible with organic dyes. The biocompatible liposomes are effectively trans- ported to the nucleus, due to the presence of CPP i.e. TAT and small size. The DOX-loaded CDs/TAT@NBLs shows improved therapeuticobserved in the cells, which clearly shows that CDs is very impor- tant to visualize the cells, in the absence of any dye. Similarly, the role of TAT was also explored using liposome prepared with CDs in the absence of TAT (CDs@NBLs). The confocal images recorded with CDs@NBLs were shown in the Fig. S12. Here, the colors were well observed in the cells; however, their intensity was very low in comparison to the images shown for CDs/TAT@NBLs in Fig. 5. This could be attributed to the less penetration of liposomes to the cells in the absence of cell penetrating peptide. The study confirms the role of CDs as well as TAT in the proper confocal imaging of MCF-7 cells. 3.10. Intercellular uptake of DOX This investigation was performed to visualize the accumulation C E D3.9. Study the optical imaging ability of liposomes After confirming the strong fluorescence and tunable emission properties of the prepared liposomes, the MCF-7 cells were selected as a probe to evaluate the optical cellular imaging function of the liposomes. Under the irradiation of the laser at a different wavelength, the CDs/TAT@NBLs was incubated with MCF-7 cells. The liposome produced bright fluorescence colors that can light up the MCF-7 cells in multi-color forms (Fig. 5-A to D). The stability of shown color (red) was also verified, after a continuous irradia- tion with the excitation laser at 810 nm for 30 min and 60 min (Fig. 5-E and F). No change in the fluorescent intensity was observed, which suggest that CDs/TAT@NBLs have excellent photo- stability and can be used for long-term cellular imaging. In addition, to explore the role of CDs in this study, the liposome Journal 312 (2017) 144ГўвӮ¬вҖң157 155efficacy, prolonged stability, and effectively to kill tumor cells in comparison to the traditional liposomes or free DOX. Therefore, the proposed liposome with good biocompatibility, low cytotoxic- ring156 E. Roy et al. / Chemical Engineeity, excellent solubility, and stable photoluminescence are a promising multifunctional platform capable of accelerating the targeted delivery of drugs in a simple and efficient manner with cell-killing approach via hyperthermia treatment using simple NIR-source. head.ce:title: RETRACTED: Carbon dot/TAT peptide co-conjugated bubble nanoliposome for multicolor cell imaging, nuclear-targeted delivery, and chemo/photothermal synergistic therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article has been retracted at the request of the Editor following concerns raised by a reader.
Figure 1F is composed of copies of the same set of nine particles of various orientations.

Also the article uses an electron micrograph in Figure 2A which represents a different part of the same image shown in Figure 3B from the paper published by the authors in вҖңBiosensors and BioelectronicsвҖқ 86 (2016) 818вҖ“826 http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2016.07.084 while claiming to represent an entirely different type of nanomaterial. The bottom of Figure 2A corresponds to the top of Figure 3B. The explanation provided by the corresponding author for this similarity was considered not satisfactory by the Editor.
These problems with the data cast doubt on all the data, and accordingly also the conclusions based on that data, in this publication.

As such this article represents a severe abuse of the scientific publishing system. The scientific community takes a very strong view on this matter and apologies are offered to readers of the journal that this was not detected during the submission process.
